_note: Campaign-authored backstory + appearance overrides keyed by DDB character ID. Loaded and merged on top of the DDB pull at character service start and on every reload. Overrides win per-field; fields not present here pass through from DDB unchanged. Survives every DDB sync without special handling on the DDB side. Edit here, or delete the key to let DDB content reassert. The _backstoryLastEditedBy marker identifies the author of the current content.

backstory.personalityTraits: I am always calm, no matter what the situation. I never raise my voice or let my emotions control me.
backstory.ideals: Honor. I don’t steal from others in the trade. (Lawful)
backstory.bonds: Someone I loved died because of a mistake I made. That will never happen again.
backstory.flaws: An innocent person is in prison for a crime that I committed. I’m okay with that.
backstory.backstoryText:
Born Radomír in a Slovak-speaking village in the Váh valley, to a cobbler named Janko and a woman named Dorota who kept chickens and sold eggs at market. When he was nine, the local lord's fifteen-year-old son beat his older sister badly enough that she did not recover. There was no court, no recourse. The village buried her. Three years later, he put rat poison in the lord's son's cup at a harvest feast. The boy died over two days. Nobody suspected the cook's boy.

He left home at fifteen and has not been back. Has not spoken to his parents in twenty years. Does not know if they are alive. Does not ask.

He has been a contract killer for eleven years. He works through brokers, most consistently through someone he has never met whom he thinks of only as 'The Clerk,' who operates out of the old Jewish Quarter in Prague via dead drops in the pew psalters at the Church of St. Henry's. He is good at his work for one reason: he disappears. Between jobs he is a cook, a stablehand, a field hand, a journeyman shoer. He actually can do these things. People remember the servant who was there when something happened. They do not remember his face.

He was taught poisons by a widow in Pest when he was nineteen. She demonstrated by letting him watch a lord's wife die across four evenings of perfectly ordinary-tasting broth. She taught him that the trick is not finding the poison. The trick is finding the dish the target cannot refuse. She vanished a year later, shop emptied overnight, no body, no forwarding. He has never looked for her.

Two months ago in Prague a contract went sideways. The target died as ordered, but one of the household guards saw him leaving by the kitchen stair. He left Prague within the hour and has been moving east ever since, taking small jobs for bed and bread, staying one town ahead of whatever sketch the guard has by now given to whomever the household sent looking.

Three days ago he reached the Pallid Hart — a crossroads inn in the Orava mountains where four roads converge and nobody asks questions. Tomas the innkeeper needed a pair of hands in the stable for the week. Vaelthion offered. Tomas asked his name. Vaelthion gave him Vaelthion. Tomas did not press. For three days now he has lived in the stable loft, taken his meals with the other staff, shoveled out stalls, and watched.

He believes he is here because the weather and the geography funneled him here. The road east runs through the Carpathians, the Carpathians run through crossroads, and this is a crossroads. He felt, four days ago, a kind of certainty that he should stop at the next inn rather than push through to the town. He did not question the certainty because he has learned to trust the instinct that has kept him alive. He should not have trusted it this time. It was not his instinct. Something else chose this inn for him the way the blizzard chose it for the others.
backstory.allies:
THE CLERK (Prague) — His broker. Never met in person; communicates via dead drops. Has not contacted Vaelthion since Prague went bad. Vaelthion does not know whether this means he is being protected or abandoned.

THE WOMAN IN PEST — His poison teacher from fifteen years ago. Vanished. May be alive. Would be approximately sixty-three now. Owes him nothing; he owes her everything.

No one else. This is not modesty. He has made sure of it.
backstory.organizations: INFORMAL BROKER NETWORK — A loose web of middlemen across Prague, Vienna, Kraków, Pest, and Bratislava. Vaelthion works primarily through The Clerk in Prague but has completed jobs via two others in the past. None of them know each other. None of them know his real name.
backstory.enemies:
THE PRAGUE HOUSEHOLD — Name unknown to him; they knew the target he killed two months ago. A household guardsman (mid-twenties, scar on his jaw, left-handed) saw him on the kitchen stair. A sketch exists. Someone has been hired to act on that sketch. He does not know who. He is moving east to keep ahead of them.

HIMSELF — Eleven years of work. No ghosts yet, literal or otherwise. He is not superstitious. He is starting to wonder whether he should be.
backstory.otherNotes:
ALIGNMENT — TRUE NEUTRAL: Confirmed by player. He is a man of his word (the one lawful element he lives by) and a killer for hire, but he has no cruelty and no ideology. He does not hate his targets and does not enjoy the work. Starting neutral gives the Phantom arc genuine runway — the moral drift across the campaign has somewhere to go from here.

COVER IDENTITIES IN ROTATION: Cook (his strongest), stablehand (currently), shoer's apprentice, wandering tinker, journeyman laundryman. He has never used the same name twice in the same region. On contracts he sometimes uses 'Tobias' or 'Janko'; between contracts he uses Vaelthion, which is the one name he actually answers to.

LANGUAGES — SLOVAK IS LOAD-BEARING: He is the only PC in the campaign tonight who understands Slovak fluently. Gregor speaks only Slovak. Marta switches to Slovak when frightened. Brother Dominik whispers to Ed in Slovak believing no one else understands. This is intentional and central to Session 0 narrative structure. Vaelthion's instinct is NOT to advertise his Slovak fluency; he keeps informational edges hidden until they are worth spending. Player should decide at table when to reveal this.

THE POISON KIT (3 custom doses in his pack, wrapped in oiled leather under the false bottom):
  • STABLEHAND'S REMEDY — Basic poison, weapon-coated, DC 10 Con save or 1d4 poison. One dose. Bitter almond smell. His everyday working stock.
  • WIDOW'S QUIET — Ingested, slow-acting. DC 13 Con save or 2d6 poison + 1 exhaustion, effect manifests 3–4 hours after ingestion, DC 14 Medicine to identify after the fact. One dose. Colorless, odorless, tasteless. For deniable kills.
  • THE MERCIFUL — Ingested, fast-acting. DC 15 Con save or incapacitated 1 minute (unconscious at 0 if failed by 5+) + 3d6 poison. On success: 1d6 poison, no unconsciousness. One dose. Pale yellow, bitter — masked by strong wine. His most expensive preparation. He does not carry it casually.
Refreshing or preparing doses requires his Poisoner's Kit and an herbalist's shop nearby. He cannot manufacture more at the Pallid Hart.

THREE DAYS AT THE PALLID HART — WHAT HE HAS ALREADY OBSERVED (information edge over other PCs at Session Zero):
  • Marta is afraid of the cellar. She does not go down. She watches the door when Tomas does. She changes the subject when guests ask about it.
  • Tomas drinks alone in the kitchen in the afternoons. He stops by sundown. That is not social drinking; that is medicating.
  • The hooded man in the corner (unknown name) has been there two evenings running. Orders bread and wine. Bread sits untouched. Wine empties — but Vaelthion has never actually seen him drink, only put the cup to his lips every 9–11 minutes. He is performing the act of eating.
  • There is a second cellar door, outside, behind the woodshed. Fresh scuff marks. Someone has been using it.
  • The goat in the shed was fine yesterday. This morning it was dead. Vaelthion examined it while mucking the stable. Wound pattern is wrong — not predator, not cut. Something softer. He has not told anyone.
  • Three-toed tracks in the snow this morning, circling the stable. Not wolf. Not deer. He does not know what made them. It scared him, and he does not scare easily.
  • Gregor the old farmer has been at the inn since before Vaelthion arrived. Speaks only Slovak. Watches everything. Knows something. No one else here speaks Slovak, so no one has asked him.

CURRENT PLAN (will not survive contact with tonight): To leave in the morning via the south road, reach the Abbey of Saint Prokop, stay one night as a traveler, take a fresh set of clothes from the monks' charity basket, then press east into the mountains.

WHY HE IS ACTUALLY HERE (the entity-pull framing): Four days ago, on the road, he felt a kind of certainty that he should stop at the next inn rather than push through. He trusted the instinct — instincts have kept him alive for eleven years. He should not have trusted it this time. Something else was choosing for him. He will not understand this for a long while, possibly not until Act 3. Each of the other PCs has their own version of this; they will eventually compare stories. That comparison is a campaign beat.

VOICE AND BEARING: Quiet. Economical. Listens more than speaks. Eats with his back to a wall and a sightline to the door — does not notice he is doing it. Will not drink anything he did not watch being poured. Sleeps in bursts of 2–3 hours, never a full night. When genuinely startled — not threatened, startled — he laughs a short, surprised laugh, and does not know why.

HORROR ARC PROFILE: Low Horror → calm, professional, slightly better at the work. Rising → quieter, stops eating with the group, notices faces in crowds that remind him of past targets. High → the Wails from the Grave start to trigger outside combat; voices in Slovak; the voices know his sister's name. Breaking Point → he goes still, speaks to no one, responds only to direct questions. The Phantom absorbs him.

PLAYER NOTE (FrostyCritter): You are the Session Zero investigation anchor. Slovak is the key; Gregor's dying words, Dominik's private whispers, Marta's frightened mutters all route to you. Do not play him as a closet hero. Let the neutrality be real. The arc earns its shape by starting from a man who honestly does not care and then finding out he does, by degrees, too late.

backstory.personalityTraits: I am always the funniest voice in the room, because if I am not, someone is going to look at me too closely. I cry when I drink, and I drink most nights.
backstory.ideals: Craft. A song finished well outlasts the patron who paid for it. (Neutral)
backstory.bonds: My niece Jane is the only person alive who calls me uncle. If the coin I send her ever stops, she will understand me to be dead. I would rather be dead than be the reason she is hungry.
backstory.flaws: I cannot feel pain anywhere except my left shoulder. I have learned to act like I do. I forget, sometimes, and I react wrong, and people notice.
backstory.backstoryText:
Chazz Mortimer was born in a hedge barn outside Frankfurt in the year 1187 to a serving-woman named Edda who did not say who the father was. The midwives saw him, said two prayers, and left. Edda raised him in the kitchen of a minor knight's hall for eleven years by telling everyone he had a disease of the skin. Then the knight died and Chazz left for the road with his mother's pitch-pipe and a stolen cloak.

By twenty he was the Nightingale of the Vltava — a voice that sang at weddings in Prague, coronations in Buda, and the naming-feasts of Wettin dukes who paid well to pretend they had not seen his skin. He learned languages the way other men learn tavern brawls: Latin first, then the Middle German of the western courts, then Czech, Polish, Slovak, and the Magyar songs nobody south of Pozsony could sing clean. The Infernal came on its own, uninvited, mostly when he was drunk.

When he was sixty-two a margrave's wife named Agnes loved him, and when her husband suspected, she sent men to end the problem quietly. They caught him in an inn south of Eger. They tortured him for an afternoon with a pressed iron — shoulder, ribs, wrists, knees, spine — because they had been told to make it look like a robbery gone bad. They left him for dead. He was not dead. The nerve damage was complete except at the first site of the iron, his left shoulder, which screams at him constantly. Every other part of him is silence. He has learned to pretend the silence is not there.

He has been moving in smaller circles ever since. Village weddings. Funerals. Guild feasts in market towns where the guild is three men and a goat. He drinks too much. He mocks himself first and loudest. His voice, when he lets it out, is still the best any of these places have ever heard, which is why they forgive him everything.

Three weeks ago a boy courier found him in an inn outside Olomouc and handed him a folded letter sealed with no crest he knew. The letter was three lines. 'The song you never finished. Come to the Pallid Hart before the snow. Payment waits.' Signed only with a curl of ink. The song he never finished was the one he was writing the week he was tortured. He had told no one. No one alive knows it exists. He should not have come. He came.
backstory.allies:
JANE MORTIMER (Bautzen, Saxony) — His half-sister Marta's daughter; Chazz's only living kin who calls him uncle. Sixteen years old, dark-haired, literate, works at a notary's for her keep. He sends her coin via a trader named Willem who keeps three coppers of every fifty. She has never seen him.

JUNO — A milk-white cat, fourteen years old, deaf in one ear, found him on a Prague rooftop one winter and has not left. Travels in his pack. Suffers the road without complaint. Has bitten exactly one person, once, correctly.

CLEO — A mongrel hound, eight years old, missing her right ear, gentle with children, hates priests. Bought for three groats from a fruit-seller in Nürnberg who was going to drown her.

WILLEM OF REGENSBURG — The trader he uses to move coin to Jane. Not a friend. Not an enemy. A working arrangement.
backstory.organizations: THE MINSTRELS' ROAD — A loose circuit of inns, halls, and abbeys from the Rhine to the Vistula where a man with a voice can find a night's bread. Not a guild. Not an organization. A set of names that trusted men pass to each other in shared firelight.
backstory.enemies:
HOUSE OF VOGEL (Margraviate of the Ostmark) — The husband's family. Twenty-five years old, this grudge, and their long memory is famous. Chazz believes the margrave is dead but the son knows the story and has men who look.

AGNES — The margrave's wife. Might be dead, might be a nun, might still be hunting him to finish what her men failed. He does not know. He does not try to find out.
backstory.otherNotes:
AGE NOTE — TIEFLING LONGEVITY: Chazz is eighty-seven. Reads as a very weathered sixty. Tieflings can live three hundred years. He is in his middle age by his blood's reckoning. Nobody in the party has asked.

SKIN — PASSING: Crimson blotching, a bald patch where two small horn-buds never broke through. He explains it as a pox he caught as a child in the east. Most people believe him. Priests rarely do. He avoids priests.

LANGUAGES — THE TRANSLATOR: He is the most-lingual PC at the table tonight. Latin (Common), Middle High German, Czech, Polish, Slovak, Magyar, and Infernal. Ed (Vaelthion) is hiding his Slovak fluency; Chazz is openly fluent and will take most of the translation load by default. When he drinks enough he answers questions in Infernal that nobody asked.

THE LETTER: In a waxed leather sleeve at the bottom of his pack, folded once. He has reread it sixteen times. The paper is Bohemian rag — expensive. The ink sits on top of the paper rather than in it; whoever wrote it has a steady hand and a slow quill. The curl at the bottom could be an A or a V or a pen-slip. He has not decided.

SONG NOTE — THE UNFINISHED ONE: Working title 'Agnes-in-winter.' Three verses and a refrain. The fourth verse, the one he was writing when the men came, is still in his head exactly where he left it. He has never sung it aloud. He has never written it down. If anything happens in this campaign that makes him sing that verse, the song is finished and the debt named in the letter comes due. The DM should treat the fourth verse as a resource slot, like a rage or a second wind, that Chazz can spend exactly once per campaign at a moment of his choosing for an effect the DM decides.

WHAT HE HAS OBSERVED AT THE PALLID HART (first evening, information edge):
  • The hooded man drums a Moravian mourning-song on his cup with his fingertips. Wrong. Someone heard it once a long time ago and is misremembering.
  • Marta's charm at her throat and Katya the minstrel's charm are the same charm — a double-crossed ash-wood token. Village-witches against the unquiet dead. He has seen one before, in Chomutov, the night three men did not come back from the well.
  • Tomas the innkeeper sings a Carpathian wolfherder's work-song under his breath when he thinks no one is listening. The dialect is accurate. He has not said he is from the Carpathians.
  • Katya is too good to be here. She is either running from something or waiting for someone.
  • Brother Aldric the pilgrim has been moving his hand over his psalter in the shape of crosses he does not put on the page. He is praying silently, constantly.

VOICE AND BEARING: Loud, then very quiet. Tips his cup toward the room before drinking. Drinks left-handed; right hand is always closer to a weapon or a door. Sings half a line to himself when he is thinking. Laughs at his own jokes before the audience gets to.

HORROR ARC PROFILE: Low → charming, lucid, dominates the room. Rising → songs he plays start sounding like songs he has not learned; cat and dog get tense. High → drifts into Infernal mid-verse without noticing. Breaking → a voice inside him sings what he sings, and his mouth moves because it is louder than he is. If Breaking hits, Juno leaves him. Cleo stays. This is how the DM will know.

PLAYER NOTE: You are the face of the party. Lean into it. Chazz is a performer who is dying to be the man who saved the day one last time. He probably can. Keep the Infernal in the drawer until a big moment, then open it once and let it be the moment.

appearance.description: A short, hunched man in a long blue cloak gone grey with road. Face blotched crimson the way old wine stains white cloth. Yellow eyes a cat notices. Walks stiffly. Cat on one shoulder, dog at his left heel.
appearance.faith: Practical — crosses himself in company, does not believe he is heard

backstory.personalityTraits: I face problems head-on. A simple direct solution is the best path. I carry the weight of what is hidden the way other people carry a cloak; after a while I stop noticing the weight is there.
backstory.ideals: Responsibility. I do what I must and I obey authority that has earned the right to be obeyed. I have stopped obeying authority that has not. (Lawful)
backstory.bonds: Those who fight beside me are those worth dying for. My father died at sea trying to cross the world I am standing on. I owe him the attempt to see it clearly.
backstory.flaws: I would rather eat my armor than admit I was wrong. I am learning that this is not courage but I have not yet unlearned it.
backstory.backstoryText:
Zarina Firethorn was born in the year 1239 on a continent no European has a name for. Her mother's people call it the Green World. Her father called it the West and meant the word the way sailors mean heaven. Her mother was an elf of the deep forests. Her father was an English sailor named Harold Thornley, washed up half-drowned on a grey beach with his ship on the rocks and seven men still roped to his wrist, all dead. He was nineteen. Her mother's people took him in. He lived with them for twenty-six years. He married Zarina's mother in the sixth. He fathered Zarina in the ninth.

He told her stories of England and of Europe and of cities with glassmakers and universities and cathedrals whose roofs no tree could reach. When she was twelve, a ship drifted into the same grey bay her father had walked out of, a trading vessel from the Norse settlements to the far north, and he went with it, to try to cross back. He did not write; there was no way to. He did not come home; there was no way to. She accepted after four years that he was dead.

She is a half-elf. Her mother's blood gave her three gifts: long life, senses a human does not have, and the Weight — a quiet sense in her chest of what around her intends harm. Before a blow lands, she knows it is coming. Before a lie is spoken, she knows it will be. Her mother's people called this the inheritance of the oldest of them, a thing a tenth of the elves born to her line still carry. In Europe, she has learned not to talk about it.

She made the crossing east herself at eighteen, in the year 1257, on a Norse trader out of Greenland. It took eleven weeks and nearly killed her. Her father's stories had not described any of it accurately. She landed at a Flemish port, worked for a year to learn the languages, and then took soldier's wages with a lesser count named Walram of Oudenaarde because he paid immediately and did not ask what she was. The Weight made her good at the work. She rose to captain of his guard in four years and stayed there for twelve.

Two years ago Walram arranged a marriage between his youngest daughter, Mathilde, and a Styrian baron. Zarina commanded the escort east. A day out of Vienna the Weight turned into a chest-heavy ache she had never felt before. She searched the party that night and found a woman named Cateline in the bridal retinue with two vials sewn into the hem of her veil. Cateline was Walram's poisoner. The ache had been the whole wedding party. The poisoning was to be Mathilde and the baron both, at the wedding feast, and the war was supposed to follow. Zarina reported this directly to Walram. Walram had sent the poisoner.

He did not kill her. He exiled her east under a fiction: soldier-for-hire sent to serve Christian kings against the eastern heathen and earn a pardon by her service. She understood the fiction when it was offered. She took it because killing him in his hall would have finished Mathilde's life faster than the poison would have.

She has been traveling east for a year. The pardon will never come. She has not pretended otherwise to herself for months. She stays east because since Flanders the Weight has been pulling her this way and the pull has only gotten stronger. Four days ago it turned into an ache again, the first time since Vienna. She rode for a day and a half without sleeping. She arrived at the Pallid Hart six hours ago. She has not taken off her armor.
backstory.allies:
HAROLD THORNLEY (deceased, presumed) — Her father. English, Yorkshireman, ship's carpenter out of King's Lynn. Dead at sea in 1251 attempting to return to Europe. She carries his bone-handled knife.

MATHILDE OF OUDENAARDE — Walram's youngest daughter, now the Baroness of Graz. Zarina saved her life and she knows it. Corresponds once a year in careful formal Latin. Will open a door for her in Styria if Zarina ever asks. Zarina has not asked.

HER MOTHER'S NAME — She will not say it aloud in Europe, because the naming is part of the bond and she believes across such a distance the bond cannot hold. She says the name only in prayer, silently, once at sunrise and once at sunset. She has not missed a day in seventeen years.
backstory.organizations: NONE — She broke her oath to the House of Oudenaarde and has not sworn another. She would swear again if she found authority worth the oath. She has not found it in Europe.
backstory.enemies:
WALRAM OF OUDENAARDE — Count of a minor Flemish house. Not hunting her. Would prefer her quietly dead but has never spent the coin. She believes this is because her Weight gave her things on him he does not want dragged into court. She was careful about what she hinted at before she left.

CATELINE — The poisoner. Flanders-Antwerp-Brabant circuit, mid-thirties, burn-scar on her right forearm. Zarina let her walk away that night because killing her in the camp would have exposed the whole fiction. Cateline knows she knows. They have not met since.
backstory.otherNotes:
THE WEIGHT — HOW IT WORKS: A quiet sensation in her chest of proximate harm. Unmarked hostility feels like cold pressure. Imminent violence feels like a fist opening inside her ribs. A silent lie in a room feels like a fly against the back of her neck. The sensation does not tell her where from, only that it is. She has spent her life learning to read the direction by walking in a slow spiral and noting the gradient. Mechanically this is her Mark of Detection — DM should hand her passive readings as free information ('a cold pressure from your left') rather than asking her to roll. She can also spend a reaction to ask one yes/no about what is under the surface of a conversation or a place. The DM answers truthfully.

ELVISH — DIALECT MISMATCH: Her Elvish is the dialect of her mother's people. It is intelligible with European Elvish the way Old English is intelligible with Middle High German — not. If she attempts to speak Elvish with a European elf, flag the DM. The languages share roots and a handful of words. Most of the rest is parallel evolution. This will be narrative grist the first time it matters.

MARK OF DETECTION — VLADISLAV: Vladislav has not been Marked by any system she has heard of. Her Weight registers him as a hole in the room. The absence is the information. He can probably feel her looking; he can definitely see that she knows.

WHAT SHE HAS OBSERVED AT THE PALLID HART (six hours, Weight-assisted):
  • The cellar door. The gradient points there. Something below the floor is the source of the ache that brought her here.
  • The hooded stranger is a hole in the room. She has never experienced this and does not know what it means. She is wary of him without animosity; she will not attack first.
  • Marta's fear-response is the pattern of someone under the long-term influence of a controller. Not a person controlling her, a situation. Zarina has seen it in camp wives whose husbands beat them and in squires whose knights wanted too much. Whatever controls Marta is below them.
  • Tomas has a fever his skin does not acknowledge. His body is hot where a healthy body is not. The heat is under the skin and not on it.
  • The old farmer Gregor, when she walked in, touched his forehead in a gesture her mother's people used when greeting a stranger whose face they recognized from a dream. He would not know the gesture. He did. She has not asked him about it yet.

ARMOR: She sleeps in her armor three nights in four. At the Pallid Hart she has not taken it off. She will not, until the ache stops.

VOICE AND BEARING: Direct. Short sentences. Does not use courtesy words when they are not meant. Sits with her back to a wall. Keeps her helmet within reach. Does not drink the first cup offered to her by someone she does not know. Speaks slowly when she is not sure of the word and accepts the correction without embarrassment.

HORROR ARC PROFILE: Low → focused, efficient, good at the work. Rising → the Weight becomes localized, she can point to threats in a room. High → sight: she sees the deaths that have happened in a building layered over the present. Breaking → she talks to one of the dead and it answers in English, in her father's voice.

PLAYER NOTE: You have the strongest information edge at the table besides Ed. The Weight is live from the moment you walk in; treat everything the DM whispers about 'a cold pressure from X direction' as free intel. You also hold one free yes/no question per scene about what is under the surface. Use it. Zarina is not a mystic; she treats her gift the way a swordswoman treats a sword — maintained, sharp, ordinary. The first time she sees something no European framework explains, she will be calmer about it than anyone expects.

appearance.description: Tall for a half-elf, olive-skinned, amber-eyed, brown hair cut short under the helmet. Father's English longsword at her left hip. A bone-handled knife at her right. She has not taken off her mail since she arrived.
appearance.faith: Her mother's — a forest-naming prayer at sunrise and sunset

backstory.personalityTraits: I place no stock in wealthy or well-mannered folk. Money and manners will not save you from a hungry thing in the dark. I am slow to trust strangers but I love the ones I love with my whole chest.
backstory.ideals: Change. Life is like the seasons, in constant change, and we must change with it. The Europeans who took me do not understand this and it is going to end them. (Chaotic)
backstory.bonds: My mother and father are in a cell under an Italian abbey. Every mission I walk away from alive keeps the lock on that cell closed. Every mission I refuse opens it. I know what the Order is. I keep going anyway, for the lock.
backstory.flaws: I rage when I should not and I cannot always feel the line before I cross it. Afterwards I remember fragments. Sometimes the fragments include faces of people I did not mean to kill.
backstory.backstoryText:
Marfire was born in the year 1254 in the deep-forest of her people's country, on the western coast of a continent no European has mapped. Her clan calls it the Green World. Her mother's people and Zarina's mother's people are not the same folk, but they share a forest and a tongue and a handful of common words with each other that are older than either language; neither woman knows this yet.

Marfire is firbolg — seven-and-a-half feet, blue-skinned, fur on her shoulders and the back of her neck, antler-nubs under her hair, ears pointed differently than any European elf's. She is twenty years old, a child by her clan's reckoning, and the only one of her kind who has ever crossed the Atlantic. She did not cross it by choice.

Four years ago, in the autumn of 1270, a Cistercian expedition landed briefly on the coast of the Green World. Twenty monks, a soldier-escort of eighteen, a Norse pilot named Rögnvaldr who had been to the edge of Vinland twice. They stayed six weeks. They traded hammered copper for pelts, salt for smoked fish, a bell for a carved idol they promptly burned on the ship. On the fifth week the abbot, a thin man named Father Girard, saw Marfire. He saw her size, her skin, her antler-nubs, and decided she was a wonder of God. He had her seized. Her clan resisted. Her parents led the resistance and were beaten down and chained. Eleven of her clan died on the beach. Father Girard negotiated: her clan would be released if Marfire came freely. She came. Her parents were released into a wooden stockade near the shore under Cistercian custody and she boarded the ship.

The voyage took fourteen weeks. She did not eat for two. She learned enough Latin by Greenland to follow Mass without being told to. She arrived in Italy on St. Nicholas's day 1270 and was paraded before the Pope in chains that had been gilded for the occasion. The Pope laid hands on her head. She did not fight it; her parents were still in the stockade.

She was sent to the Abbey of the Seven Flames in the Apennines — a militant Cistercian offshoot founded the century before to hunt heretics in the German east — and given a new name, Marfire the Converted. The Order presented her to donor-kings as their great conversion trophy: the giant-woman of the Western Savage Peoples, brought to Christ, now a sword of the Order. She has been in their service for three years eight months. She has killed six people on their behalf. All six were peasants the Order accused of heresy. None of the six were heretics. Her parents are still in the stockade. The Order sends proof of life every feast-day; the proofs are two sentences each, in a Cistercian hand, and they do not include her parents' words.

Her handler is Brother Ignatius — a thin Burgundian with a scar running temple to jaw — who assigns her missions and keeps her under a vow of silence about the Order in public. Ten days ago Brother Ignatius told her there was a werewolf loose in the Orava mountains and the Order wanted it ended. She knew he was lying. The lie was not about the werewolf; the lie was about what else the Order wanted. She believes the Order now wants her dead by something large enough to make her death convincing. She is more trouble than trophy to them. The trophy story is wearing thin. The next Pope may want the story to simply end.

She took the mission. She has been riding east for nine days. Yesterday, in a stand of oaks an hour's ride from the Pallid Hart, one of the trees whispered to her. European trees do not whisper. That tree knew her name. She came to the Pallid Hart to see what else in this valley was not what it pretended to be.
backstory.allies:
HER MOTHER (name withheld in Europe, custody at the Abbey of the Seven Flames) — Leader of her clan's resistance on the beach four years ago. In the stockade at the Abbey. She is why Marfire keeps walking missions.

HER FATHER (name withheld, same custody) — Same stockade. Same reason. Marfire has not heard his voice in four years.

RÖGNVALDR THE PILOT — The Norse sailor who guided the Cistercian expedition. Did not participate in her capture. Visited her in her cell the night before her first voyage and taught her to say 'I am a guest' in seven European languages. She does not know if he is alive. She has his broken ivory amulet.

NO ONE ELSE IN EUROPE — She has had no friend since she crossed the ocean. She does not know whether to trust the PCs at this inn. She wants to.
backstory.organizations: THE ORDER OF THE SEVEN FLAMES — Cistercian militant offshoot, Apennine mother-house, chapters in Cologne, Kraków, and Esztergom. Officially crusaders against heresy in the east. In practice, a body of well-funded killers with papal cover. She belongs to them on paper. She has not yet refused an assignment. Tonight may be the night.
backstory.enemies:
BROTHER IGNATIUS OF BURGUNDY — Her handler. Scar temple to jaw. Pale, thin, moves quietly. Has not laid a hand on her; does not need to. She suspects he travels with two armored brothers in the next village waiting to hear that she has died heroically.

FATHER GIRARD (deceased) — The abbot who seized her four years ago. Died of a fever in Milan the year after, according to the Order's chronicles. She believes this. She does not grieve.

THE ORDER OF THE SEVEN FLAMES (institutional) — They own her on parchment. They do not love her. They will not mourn her.
backstory.otherNotes:
APPEARANCE IN EUROPE: Nobody in 1274 Europe has seen a firbolg before. Seven-and-a-half feet, blue-skinned, gray fur at shoulders and neck, antler-nubs under her hair, ears pointed differently from European elves. Walking into a European village is a story the village will tell for two generations. She has learned to walk in at night and to travel hooded when she can. She has not been able to hide, ever.

LANGUAGES — DIALECT NOTES: Latin (Common) — learned on the voyage and at the Abbey, serviceable, not literary. Elvish — her mother's dialect, same issue as Zarina's: not mutually intelligible with European Elvish. Giant — the common giant-tongue of her mother's forests; European giants speak a cousin of it. Speech of Beast and Leaf — her real inheritance. Works on all animals everywhere. Works on trees but European trees answer her in the wrong rhythm, slow and off-meter, as if they are translating. Yesterday's oak was the first European tree that did not.

THE ORDER'S COLLAR: A hammered iron torc around her neck, blessed by the Abbot General, engraved with the Seven Flames. She is required to wear it at all times under her clothing. It is a signal object; brothers of the Order can identify each other by seeing it, and it is her guarantee that her parents are alive. Removing it is the act that ends the arrangement. She has touched it six times a day for three years. She will remove it when she is ready to let the Order kill her parents. The campaign is built for that moment.

WHY SHE IS ACTUALLY HERE (entity-pull): The Order sent her on a lie. The real reason she came is the tree yesterday. Something in this valley is not what it is pretending to be and it knew her name in a language that should not exist on this side of the ocean. She will not understand the pull completely until late in the campaign. She will understand it earlier than the other PCs.

WHAT SHE HAS OBSERVED AT THE PALLID HART (arrived this morning):
  • The door is too small; she had to duck sideways. Everyone stared. She is used to this.
  • The hooded stranger looked at her and did not look away. Nobody in Europe has ever not looked away. She looked back until he did not. He still has not.
  • The goat in the shed was eaten wrong. Softer teeth than a predator. Something fed on the goat that wanted only the blood. Her clan knows animals like this. Europe is not supposed to have them.
  • Marta is afraid of her and drawn to her in the same movement. Her mother's people had a word for this: the way small animals both freeze and approach a large warm thing.
  • The old farmer Gregor watched her come in and said nothing and kept stirring his soup. First European in four years to do that. She wants to sit with him.
  • The cellar door has three curved parallel scratches low on the jamb — not claw, more like a signature. Nobody else in the room has noticed them.

VOICE AND BEARING: Quiet in crowds. Loud when she laughs, which is rare in Europe. Eats with her fingers when she is alone and with a knife when she is not. Does not lie well. Does not try. When she rages her eyes go the color of riverwater.

HORROR ARC PROFILE: Low → grounded, reads the forest and the room, trusts her hands. Rising → the European trees begin whispering wrong, overlapping, in languages she does not know. High → her rages get longer; she comes out of them with blood she cannot remember earning and fragments of faces. Breaking → she hears her mother calling from across the ocean, clearly, in her own voice, saying her real name. If the Breaking hits she will walk into the Atlantic to follow the voice. The campaign is built for this moment too.

PLAYER NOTE: You are the warm heart of the party and you are twenty years old. Do not armor Marfire with cynicism; let her care openly, even with strangers. Her Speech of Beast and Leaf is live from scene one — if the DM describes an animal, you can ask what it feels. Her Elvish is her mother's dialect — flag the DM if you try to speak it with a European elf. Your parents are hostages. Every mission makes you more obedient until the moment it doesn't. The Order's torc stays on until you choose to take it off. The DM will know what that moment means when it comes. So will you.

appearance.description: Seven-and-a-half feet tall, blue-skinned, gray fur along the shoulders and the back of the neck. Antler-nubs under dark hair. Lavender eyes. Ears pointed differently from any European elf. An iron torc of the Order of the Seven Flames under her cloak at the collarbone; do not show to strangers.
appearance.faith: Her clan's forest-prayer, said silently; wears the Order's cross over it
